title: Cat Watery Eyes and Sneezing: Causes and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat sneezing with watery eyes? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and how to help your pet with our triage guide.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sneezing and watery eyes in cats are typically caused by mild upper respiratory infections similar to a human cold; seek veterinary care if your cat stops eating or if discharge turns yellow or green.

What causes my cat to have sneezing and watery eyes?

When a cat exhibits sneezing combined with watery eyes, it is most commonly a sign of a feline upper respiratory infection (URI). Much like a common cold in humans, these infections are frequently viral, often caused by Feline Herpesvirus-1 or Feline Calicivirus. Other possibilities include environmental allergies to dust, pollen, or strong fragrances, and occasionally, mild bacterial infections.

How do I know if my cat's sneezing and watery eyes require an emergency visit?

  • If your cat is active, eating well, and the eye discharge remains clear, the urgency level is low.
  • Symptoms often resolve with supportive care or minor medical intervention.
  • Contact your veterinarian for a non-emergency appointment within 24 to 48 hours if your cat stops eating or becomes lethargic.
  • Seek veterinary care if the eye discharge changes from clear to yellow or green.

How does taking a photo of my cat's eyes help with the triage process?

  • A clear, close-up photo allows the veterinary team to assess the color and consistency of ocular discharge.
  • Professionals can check for swelling of the conjunctiva.
  • A photo helps identify if the third eyelid is protruding.
  • This visual evidence helps the triage assistant determine if your cat needs to be seen immediately or can be monitored at home.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Feline viral rhinotracheitis (FVR) and feline calicivirus (FCV) infections are common causes of watery eyes and sneezing in cats, with dual infections possible. Transmission occurs via aerosol droplets and fomites. The onset of FVR is marked by fever, frequent sneezing, conjunctivitis, rhinitis, and often salivation. Initially, a serous nasal and ocular discharge occurs, which soon becomes mucopurulent, accompanied by depression and anorexia. Concurrent upper respiratory inflammation can also cause lacrimation and conjunctivitis. Sneezing is frequent in acute rhinitis as the cat attempts to clear the upper airways.

Chapter: Infectious Disease, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1481)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human cold medicine?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your cat human medications. Many over-the-counter human drugs, such as acetaminophen or certain decongestants, are highly toxic to cats.

faqs.question: How can I help my cat feel better at home?
faqs.answer: You can provide relief by gently wiping away eye discharge with a warm, damp cloth and using a humidifier to help clear their nasal passages.

faqs.question: Is sneezing and watery eyes contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, upper respiratory infections are highly contagious between cats. If you have other felines in the house, it is best to keep the symptomatic cat isolated until they recover.
